narrative.anchor_matched: No experiential context — generic space with no immersive framing; pure utility setup

evidence.quote: The room has light wooden flooring and white walls. There is a black board on the right side of the room.
evidence.indicator: Thematic decor that names or evokes a setting
evidence.reasoning: The room is described as a generic space with plain walls, wooden flooring, and a blackboard — no thematic decor, props, or framing that would place children inside a deliberate setting.

evidence.quote: A teacher and five children are sitting on the floor in a circle, playing with balloons. There are two white balloons and two yellow balloons.
evidence.indicator: Props that place the child inside the theme
evidence.reasoning: Balloons are activity props for free movement play but are not framed within any narrative setting or thematic context — there is no scene-setting that places children inside a wider story.

evidence.quote: A staff member in a blue uniform enters the room from the right side. The children continue to play with the balloons. The teacher is still seated, interacting with the children. Another staff member is visible in the background, sitting at a desk.
evidence.indicator: An atmospheric anchor (banner, central feature, framing object)
evidence.reasoning: No atmospheric anchor, banner, or central feature is visible at any point; the background shows a staff member at a desk, reinforcing the generic utility nature of the space.

narrative.scorer_notes: The transcript is uninformative (repeated 'Ah' utterances throughout). Visual observations consistently describe a plain room with no thematic decor, no immersive framing, and no narrative context beyond balloons as activity props. This is a pure utility setup scoring 0.

multi_sensory.anchor_matched: Two or more senses are meaningfully engaged through deliberate material and arrangement choices

evidence.quote: A teacher and five children are sitting on the floor in a circle, playing with balloons. There are two white balloons and two yellow balloons.
evidence.indicator: Tactile invitations (textures, materials to touch, wet/dry/soft/rough)
evidence.reasoning: Balloons provide a distinct tactile experience — smooth, light, elastic — engaging touch alongside vision (color-differentiated balloons).

evidence.quote: One child in a blue dress stands up and walks around, holding a yellow balloon. Another child in a red shirt also stands up and walks around with a yellow balloon.
evidence.reasoning: Children are freely moving — standing, walking, and carrying balloons — providing proprioceptive and whole-body movement engagement alongside tactile and visual stimulation.

evidence.quote: One child in a blue shirt is crawling on the floor, pushing a white balloon.
evidence.reasoning: Crawling and pushing a balloon on the floor is a deliberate full-body movement engagement, adding a third sensory mode (proprioceptive) to the activity.

evidence.quote: There are two white balloons and two yellow balloons.
evidence.reasoning: Color-differentiated balloons provide visual variety as a deliberate design element of the activity.

multi_sensory.scorer_notes: The activity as designed (balloon dance/play) meaningfully engages at least three senses: tactile (handling balloons), proprioceptive/movement (walking, crawling, dancing with balloons), and visual (colorful balloons in open space). The transcript is uninformative (only 'Ah' utterances), but the activity notes and visual observations provide sufficient convergent evidence. Music is mentioned in the activity notes but not confirmed in the transcript or visuals; even without it, three senses are clearly engaged.

boundary.anchor_matched: A boundary is implied but ambiguous (e.g. materials present but no visible zone marker; or unclear where the activity area starts and stops)

evidence.quote: A teacher and five children are sitting on the floor in a circle, playing with balloons. There are two white balloons and two yellow balloons. The children are wearing casual clothes. The room has light wooden flooring and white walls. There is a black board on the right side of the room.
evidence.indicator: A clear physical or visual marker defining the activity area
evidence.reasoning: The circle formation on the floor implies a loose activity zone, but there is no mat, rug, tape, or other explicit boundary marker — the wooden floor is undifferentiated throughout the room.

evidence.quote: One child in a blue dress stands up and walks around, holding a yellow balloon. Another child in a red shirt also stands up and walks around with a yellow balloon. The teacher remains seated, interacting with the children.
evidence.indicator: The boundary is intuitive to a child without verbal explanation from an adult
evidence.reasoning: Children freely walk around the open room with balloons, indicating no clear boundary is constraining or defining the activity zone — the space bleeds into the broader room.

evidence.quote: A staff member in a blue uniform enters the room from the right side. The children continue to play with the balloons. The teacher is still seated, interacting with the children. Another staff member is visible in the background, sitting at a desk.
evidence.indicator: A clear physical or visual marker defining the activity area
evidence.reasoning: The presence of a desk and staff in the background with no visible divider or zone marker confirms the activity area is not separated from the rest of the room, making the boundary ambiguous.

boundary.scorer_notes: The seated circle provides an implied social boundary, but no physical or visual zone marker (mat, rug, tape, etc.) is visible. Children move freely beyond the circle into the open room, and the activity space is not clearly delineated from the broader classroom environment. Scored 0.5 as boundary is implied but not unambiguous.

movement.anchor_matched: Both elements are present — children can clearly access the activity zone (visible path markers not required; minor adult presence in the path is acceptable as long as children get past) AND deliberate positioning suited to the activity (standing, kneeling, or sitting as the activity calls for), with materials reachable from that posture. Explicit seating like cushions is NOT required when the activity naturally invites standing or kneeling.

evidence.quote: A teacher and five children are sitting on the floor in a circle, playing with balloons. There are two white balloons and two yellow balloons. The children are wearing casual clothes. The room has light wooden flooring and white walls.
evidence.indicator: Clear deliberate positioning suited to the activity
evidence.reasoning: The open floor space with children seated/moving in a circle is appropriate positioning for a balloon dance/movement activity, with balloons directly in hand — materials reachable from the natural posture.

evidence.quote: One child in a blue dress stands up and walks around, holding a yellow balloon. Another child in a red shirt also stands up and walks around with a yellow balloon. The teacher remains seated, interacting with the children.
evidence.indicator: Enough space at the activity for children to stand, kneel, sit, or move as the activity requires
evidence.reasoning: Children are freely standing up and walking around with balloons, demonstrating sufficient open floor space for the movement activity.

evidence.quote: One child in a blue shirt is crawling on the floor, pushing a white balloon. Another child in a red shirt is sitting on the floor, holding a yellow balloon. The teacher is interacting with the children, helping them with the balloons.
evidence.indicator: Materials reachable from the natural posture the activity invites
evidence.reasoning: Children are engaging with balloons from multiple postures (crawling, sitting, standing) without any obstruction, confirming materials are reachable and space is unobstructed.

evidence.quote: A staff member in a blue uniform enters the room from the right side. The children continue to play with the balloons. The teacher is still seated, interacting with the children. Another staff member is visible in the background, sitting at a desk.
evidence.indicator: Children can clearly access the activity zone from the room entry — minor adult presence in the path is acceptable as long as children can still get past
evidence.reasoning: An additional staff member enters and observes but children continue playing freely, indicating adult presence does not obstruct child movement or access to the activity zone.

movement.scorer_notes: The activity is a free-movement balloon dance on an open wooden floor. Children are observed standing, sitting, crawling, and walking freely throughout the session. The open floor layout clearly supports the movement activity. Adult presence (teacher seated, additional staff member entering and observing) does not materially obstruct child movement.

clean_up.scorer_notes: The entire 5-minute recording captures only the active play phase of a balloon dance activity. No cleanup transition occurs, no storage locations are visible, and no return of materials is observed. Balloons are inherently lightweight and child-manageable, but the session provides no evidence about storage height, labelling, return locations, or whether children can complete cleanup unaided. There is insufficient evidence to score this dimension.
